standard_description: Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table), and explain them using properties of operations. For example, observe that 4 times a number is always even, and explain why 4 times a number can be decomposed into two equal addends.
subject: Math

Task
Below is a table showing all the ways to add the numbers from 1 to 9.
Each sum which is larger than 10 can be found by first making a 10. For example, to find 8 + 5, we can write
\begin{align} 8+5 &= 8 + (2+3) \\ &= (8+2) + 3 \\ &= 10 + 3 \\ &= 13. \end{align}
Explain why this reasoning works and apply this method to find 7 + 8. How can you visualize these equations using the table?
Adding 9 to another single digit number can also be done by first making a 10. For example
\begin{align} 3+9 &= 3 + (10-1) \\ &= (3+10) - 1 \\ &= 13 - 1 \\ &= 12. \end{align}
Explain why this reasoning works and apply this method to find 7+9. How can you visualize these equations using the table?

Students are expected to be fluent with addition and subtraction within 20 in second
grade (see 2.OA.2). This task builds on that knowledge by asking them to study more
carefully the make-a-ten strategy that they should already know and use intuitively. In
this strategy, knowledge of which sums make a ten, together with some of the
properties of addition and subtraction, are used to evaluate sums which are larger
than 10. The 10's in the table are shaded because they are essential to the technique.
The second part of the task focuses on adding 9's which can also be accomplished by

purposes as it takes time to identify the patterns involved and understand the steps in
the procedures.
The Standards for Mathematical Practice focus on the nature of the learning
experiences by attending to the thinking processes and habits of mind that students
need to develop in order to attain a deep and flexible understanding of mathematics.
Certain tasks lend themselves to the demonstration of specific practices by students.
The practices that are observable during exploration of a task depend on how
instruction unfolds in the classroom. While it is possible that tasks may be connected to
several practices, only one practice connection will be discussed in depth. Possible
secondary practice connections may be discussed but not in the same degree of detail. 
This task helps illustrate Mathematical Practice 7, Look for and make use of structure.

IM Commentary
This task is intended for instruction. The goal is to look for structure and identify
patterns and then try to find the mathematical explanation for this. This problem
examines the ''checkerboard'' pattern of even and odd numbers in a single digit
multiplication table. The even numbers in the table are examined in depth using a
grade appropriate notion of even, namely the possibility of reaching the number
counting by 's or expressing the number as a whole number of pairs.
The multiplication table itself is a little hard to parse because it has so much
information in it. The teacher should point out that the first row and first column are
not part of the table itself but show what numbers are being multiplied. In general, the
students will probably need a lot of help understanding what the task is asking.
Students are most likely to benefit if they can work with a partner or in small groups on
this task.

addition by examining the addition facts for single digit numbers. This is important as it
gives students a chance, at a young age, to do more than memorize these arithmetic
facts which they will use throughout their education. The approach taken here is a
geometric one, associating the commutative property of addition with the symmetry of
the addition table. Students don't "officially" study symmetry until 4th grade (see
4.G.3), so this task defines the particular symmetry for them.

The symmetry of the table about the diagonal essentially cuts in half the number of

This task is mainly intended for instructional purposes. There will be ample
opportunities to test whether or not the students have learned their single digit
addition tables and the goal of this task is to facilitate the process by revealing patterns
and structure in the table.

khan_exercises.title: Represent 2-step word problems with equations
khan_exercises.description: Represent two-step word problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Equations include addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

items.question_content:
My book is $58$ pages. I have already read $13$ pages. I plan to read $5$ pages each day until I finish the book.

**Which equation could I use to find how many days $(\blueD{d})$ it will take to finish reading the book?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
My book has $58$ pages, and I have already read $13$ pages.  

I can subtract $58 - 13$ to find out how many pages are left to read.
Next, I will divide the difference by the $5$ pages I read each day to find out how many days it will take to finish reading the book.

items.question_content:
When I started my rock collection I had $8$ rocks. Over the summer I collected $48$ additional rocks. At the end of the summer I gave $29$ of my favorite rocks to friends, but I still have some rocks left.
 
**Which equation could I use to find out how many rocks $(\blueD{r})$ I still have left?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: I can add $8+48$ to find the total number of rocks I had before giving any away.
Then, I can subtract the $29$ rocks I gave away to find the number of rocks I have left.

items.question_content:
I had $33$ pictures from summer camp. I gave $3$ pictures away to friends, and I plan to divide the remaining pictures evenly between $5$ pages of a photo album.
 
**Which equation could I use to find how many pictures  $(\blueD{p})$ will be on each page?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: I can subtract $33-3$ to find the total number of pictures that will be in the photo album.
Next, I will divide the difference by $5$ to find out how many photos will be on each page.

items.question_content:
On Monday, I bought $23$ strawberries. On Tuesday, I ate $8$ of the strawberries.  Now, I  want to divide the remaining strawberries into bags of $5$ strawberries each. 

**Which equation could I use to find how many bags $(\blueD{b})$ of strawberries I will have?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
I bought $23$ strawberries, and I have already eaten $8$ strawberries.  

I can subtract $23-8$ to find out how many strawberries I have left.
Next, I will divide the difference by groups of $5$ to find out how many bags of strawberries I will have.

items.question_content:
I had a container that contained $32$ ounces of flour. On Monday I added $47$ ounces of flour to the container. During the week, I made cookies and waffles with some of the flour.  At the end of the week, the container had $61$ ounces of flour remaining.  
 
**Which equation could I use to find out how many ounces $(\blueD{o})$ of flour I used during the week?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
I started with $32$ ounces of flour and addded $47$ more ounces.

I can add $32+47$ to find the total number of ounces of flour I had before using any.
Next, I can subtract the $61$ ounces of flour I had left at the end of the week from this sum to find out how many ounces of flour I used during the week.

items.question_content:
I had $\$88$. Then I earned $\$29$ walking dogs. After I spent some money for concert tickets, I have $\$74$ remaining.  
 
**Which equation could I use to find out how much money $(\blueD{m})$ I spent on concert tickets?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
I started with $\$88$ and then earned $\$29$ walking dogs. 

 I can add $88 + 29$ to find the total amount of money I had before spending any.
Next I can subtract the $\$74$ I had remaining from this sum to find out how much I spent on concert tickets.

items.question_content:
I had $\$147$. Then I spent $\$42$ on sneakers. Then, I earned $\$45$ by winning a race in my new sneakers!
 
**Which equation can I use to find out how much money $(\blueD{m})$ I have now?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
I started with $\$147$ and spent $\$42$ on sneakers.

 I can subtract $147- 42$ to find the total amount of money I have remaining.
Next, I will add the $\$45$ I earned in the race to this difference to find my new total.

items.question_content:
When I woke up, it was $26$ degrees outside. During the day, the temperature increased $17$ degrees, and then it began to decrease. When I went to bed, it was $28$ degrees.  

**Which equation can I use to find out how much the temperature $(\blueD{t})$ decreased after reaching its high point?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The temperature started at  $26$ degrees  and then increased by  $17$ degrees.  

I can add $26+ 17$ to find the highest temperature of the day.
Next, I will subtract the temperature of  $28$ degrees when I went to bed to find out how many degrees the temperature decreased after reaching its high point.

items.question_content:
Dikembe scored $8$ points during each of the first $3$ quarters of his basketball game. During the last quarter Dikembe scored $11$ points.

**Which equation can we use to find the total number of points $(\blueD{p})$ Dikembe scored during the game?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: We can multiply $3\times8$ to find the number of points Dikembe scored during the first three quarters of the game.
Then we can add the $11$ points Dikembe scored in the fourth quarter to the product to find the total number of points he scored in the game.
$8\times3+11=\blueD{p}$

items.question_content:
Nick is going to roast some marshmallows over a campfire tonight. Nick buys $4$ bags of marshmallows. Each bag costs $\$3$.  Nick also buys a $\$2$ lighter.

**Which equation can we use to find the total amount of money  $(\blueD{m})$ Nick spent?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We can find the total cost of the marshmallows by multiplying
$3\times4$.
Since Nick is buying marshmallows and a lighter, we can add the $\$2$ cost of the lighter to the cost of the marshmallows to find his total bill.
$4\times3+2= \blueD{m}$

items.question_content:
Today, a frog ate $11$ crickets for breakfast, $8$ crickets for lunch, and $12$ crickets for dinner.

**Which equation can we use to find the total number of crickets $(\blueD{c})$ the frog ate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can add $11+8$ to find the number of crickets the frog ate for breakfast and lunch.
Then, we can add the $12$ crickets the frog ate for dinner to the sum to find the total number of crickets eaten all day.

items.question_content:
During the first $4$ days of the week Lucca built $2$ robots each day. On Friday, he built $3$ robots.

**Which equation can we use to find the total number of robots $(\blueD{r})$ Lucca made during the week?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First, let's find how many robots Lucca built during the first $4$ days of the week. To do this, we can multiply $4\times2$.
Then, we can add the $3$ robots Lucca built on Friday to the product to find the total number of robots Lucca built for the week.
$4\times2+3=\blueD{r}$

items.question_content:
A turtle ran $10$ meters per minute for $7$ minutes of a race. Then she ran $55$ more meters to finish the race.

**Which equation can we use to find the total number of meters $(\blueD{m})$ in the race?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can multiply $7\times10$ to find the number of meters the turtle ran during the first $7$ minutes.
Then we can add $55$ meters to the product to find the total length of the race.
$10\times7 + 55=\blueD{m}$

items.question_content:
One day at lunch, a restaurant served  $178$ burgers, $63$ grilled cheese sandwiches, and $37$ salads.

**Estimate how many meals the restaurant served for lunch that day.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We estimate to add, subtract, multiply, and divide with friendly numbers.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

A restaurant served $\greenD{178}$ burgers.



$\greenD{178}$ is about $180$.

$\greenD{178}\approx180$

A restaurant served $\blueD{63}$ grilled cheese sandwiches.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{63}$ is about $60$.

$\blueD{63}\approx60$

A restaurant served $\purpleC{37}$ salads.



[[☃ image 1]]



$\purpleC{37}$ is about $40$.

$\purpleC{37}\approx40$

To find the total number of meals, we add:

$\greenD{180}+\blueD{60}+\purpleC{40}\approx280$
The restaurant served *about* $280$ meals for lunch.

Sal's book is $52$ pages. Sal already read $18$ pages. He plans to read $10$ pages each day until he finishes the book.

**Estimate how many days it will take Sal to finish reading the book.**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We estimate to add, subtract, multiply, and divide with friendly numbers.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Sal's book is $\greenD{52}$ pages long.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\greenD{52}$ is about $50$.

$\greenD{52}\approx50$

Sal read $\blueD{18}$ pages.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{18}$ is about $20$.

$\blueD{18}\approx20$

He plans to read $\purpleC{10}$ pages a day.

$\purpleC{10}$ is already a friendly number.

To find the number of days it will take Sal to finish reading, we subtract and then divide:

$(\greenD{50}-\blueD{20})\div\purpleC{10}\approx3$
It will take Sal *about* $3$ days to finish reading the book.

On Monday, Cody bought $41$ cherries. On Tuesday, Cody ate $19$ cherries.  Now, she wants to divide the remaining cherries into bags of $4$ cherries each. 
 
**Estimate how many bags of $4$ cherries Cody can make.**

hints:

$(\greenD{40}-\blueD{20})\div\purpleC{4}\approx5$
Cody can make *about* $5$ bags of cherries.

The third grade teachers had a pizza party. They ordered $11$ large pizzas and each pizza had $8$ slices. There were $4$ classrooms in the grade.

**Estimate how many slices of pizza each classroom can have if the pizzas are shared equally.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We estimate to add, subtract, multiply, and divide with friendly numbers.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The third grade ordered $\greenD{11}$ pizzas.

[[☃ image 1]]

$(\greenD{10}\times\blueD{8})\div\purpleC{4}\approx20$
Each classroom can have *about* $20$ slices of pizza.

khan_exercises.title: Patterns in multiplication tables
khan_exercises.description: Practice discovering and explaining patterns in multiplication tables.

items.question_content:
**Fill in the blank with the correct answer choice. **   
*Hint: Use the multiplication table to help.*

![Multiplication table with the columns labeled 4 and 8 shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9de4b081ce2a1c7edbe72f0ca74ac4c7c0ff9388)

**When we multiply by $8$, we  [[☃ dropdown 1]]  get double the number we would get when we multiply by $4$.**
hints:
Doubling a number is the same as multiplying by $2$.  

Is multiplying a number, $n$, by $8$ equivalent to multiplying by $4$ and $2$?

$n\times8 \stackrel{?}{=}n \times 4 \times 2$

Let's use the table to check.

$n$ | $n\times 8$ | $n\times 4$| $(n \times 4) \times 2 \stackrel{?}{=} n \times 8$
-: |-: |-: |-: |
$1$ | $8$ | $4$ | $4 \times2 \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 8$
$2$ | $16$ | $8$ | $8 \times2\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 16$
$3$ | $24$ | $12$ | $12 \times2 \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 24$
$4$ | $32$ | $16$ | $16 \times2 \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 32$
$5$ | $40$ | $20$ | $20 \times2 \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 40$
$6$ | $48$ | $24$ | $24 \times2 \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 48$
$7$ | $56$ | $28$ | $28 \times2 \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 56$
$8$ | $64$ | $32$ | $32 \times2\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 64$
$9$ | $72$ | $36$ | $36 \times2 \stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 72$
$10$ | $80$ | $40$ | $40\times2\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 80$

For all of the factors in the table, the statement is true.

Does this work for any number? 

$\begin{align}
n \times 8 &\stackrel{?}=n \times 4 \times 2 \\\\
n \times 8 &\stackrel{?}=n \times (4 \times 2) \\\\
n \times 8&\stackrel{\checkmark}= n \times8
\end{align}$

Yes, the statement is **always** true.
When we multiply by $8$, we *always* get double the number we would get when we multiply by $4$.

items.question_content:
**Fill in the blank with the correct answer choice. **   
*Hint: Use the multiplication table to help.*

![Multiplication table with the rows labeled 2, 5, and 7 shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/283ba75c4fd30856289ab827178f7c50ed8a799c)

**When we multiply by $7$, we  [[☃ dropdown 1]]  get the sum of what we get by multiplying by $2$ and by $5$.**
hints:
If we have $\bigstar$ per group, is $7$ $\bigstar$s the same as $2$ $\bigstar$s plus $5$ $\bigstar$s?

$\bigstar\times 7 \stackrel{?}{=}\bigstar \times 2 + \bigstar \times 5$

Let's use the table to check.

$\bigstar$ | $\bigstar\times 2$ | $\bigstar\times 5$| $\bigstar \times 7$ | $\bigstar \times 2 + \bigstar \times 5\stackrel{?}{=}\bigstar\times 7 $
-: |-: |-: |-: | :-:
$1$ | $2$ | $5$ | $7$ | $2+5\stackrel{\checkmark}=7$
$2$ | $4$ | $10$ | $14$ | $4+10\stackrel{\checkmark}=14$
$3$ | $6$ | $15$ | $21$ | $6+15\stackrel{\checkmark}=21$
$4$ | $8$ | $20$ | $28$ | $8+20\stackrel{\checkmark}=28$
$5$ | $10$ | $25$ | $35$ | $10+25\stackrel{\checkmark}=35$
$6$ | $12$ | $30$ | $42$ | $12+30\stackrel{\checkmark}=42$
$7$ | $14$ | $35$ | $49$ | $14+35\stackrel{\checkmark}=49$
$8$ | $16$ | $40$ | $56$ | $16+40\stackrel{\checkmark}=56$
$9$ | $18$ | $45$ | $63$ | $18+45\stackrel{\checkmark}=63$
$10$ | $20$ | $50$ | $70$ | $20+50\stackrel{\checkmark}=70$

For all of the factors in the table, the statement is true.

Does this work for any number? 

$\begin{align}
\bigstar \times 7 &\stackrel{?}=\bigstar \times 2 + \bigstar \times 5 \\\\
\bigstar \times 7 &\stackrel{?}=\bigstar \times (2 + 5) \\\\
\bigstar \times 7 &\stackrel{\checkmark}=\bigstar \times 7
\end{align}$

Yes, the statement is **always** true.
When we multiply by $7$, we *always* get the sum of what we get by multiplying by $2$ and by $5$.

items.question_content:
**Fill in the blank with the correct answer choice. **   
*Hint: Use the multiplication table to help.*

![Multiplication table with the columns labeled 5 and 10 shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c109888a074ce53751395b021c88f825e4d1e60c)

**When we add any two multiples of $5$ and $10$, we  [[☃ dropdown 1]]  get a number ending in $2$.**
hints:
If we add a multiple of $5$, $\heartsuit$, with a multiple of $10$, $\bigstar$, do we ever get an answer with a $2$ in the ones place?

$\heartsuit+\bigstar \stackrel{?}{=} \square 2$

Does this work for any number? 

All multiples of $5$ end in $0$ and $5$. All multiples of $10$ end in $0$. 

When we add a number with $0$ in the ones place to a number with $5$ or $0$ in ones place, the sum has a $0$ or $5$ in the ones place, never a $2$.

So, this statement is *never* true.
When we add any two multiples of $5$ and $10$, we *never*  get a number ending in $2$.

items.question_content:

*Hint: Use the multiplication table to help.*

![Multiplication table with the row labeled 3 shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e38e07541b05f8775138c0392128eca8c1240d17)

**When we multiply a number by $3$, we  [[☃ dropdown 1]]  get the same value as if we added $6$ to that number.**
hints:
If we have $3$ $\heartsuit$s, is that the same as having $\heartsuit$ plus $6$ ?

$\heartsuit \times 3\stackrel{?}{=}\heartsuit + 6$

Let's use the table to check.

$\heartsuit$ | $\heartsuit\times 3$ | $\heartsuit \times 3\stackrel{?}{=}\heartsuit + 6$
-: |-: |-: |-: | :-:
$1$ | $3$ | $1+6\neq3$
$2$ | $6$ | $2+6\neq6$
$3$ | $9$ | $3+6\stackrel{\checkmark}=9$
$4$ | $12$ | $4+6\neq12$
$5$ | $15$ | $5+6\neq15$
$6$ | $18$ | $6+6\neq18$
$7$ | $21$ | $7+6\neq21$
$8$ | $24$ | $8+6\neq24$
$9$ | $27$ | $9+6\neq27$
$10$ | $30$ | $10+6\neq30$

For the factors in the table, the statement is sometimes true.
When we multiply by $3$, we *sometimes* get the same value as if we added $6$.

Part of a multiplication table is below.

**Complete the pattern in the multiplication table.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
Lets figure out the factors for the rows and columns. Then we can multiply to find the missing values.

![The same table as above, marked with a multiplication symbol.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/99f24fe0eeb1631c2351755680a7c466578f2c85)

$18 \stackrel{\maroonD{+3}}{\longrightarrow} 21 \stackrel{\maroonD{+3}}{\longrightarrow} 24$

The factor for the first row is $\maroonD{3}$.

![The same table as above, with the first row labeled 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d16d422e141c4f2d9a12539ba08c7748920d1380)

The factor for each row of a multiplication table is one more than the row above it.

![The same table as above, with the rows labeled 3, 4, and 5 respectively.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0cb5101f781088ede28f6acf233519097e8059ec)

$18 \stackrel{\blueE{+6}}{\longrightarrow} 24 \stackrel{\blueE{+6}}{\longrightarrow} 30$

The factor for the first column is $\blueE{6}$.

![The same table as above, with the first column labeled 6.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b84da0acd90dd7296f7b01d8ab8cbc63879318e6)

The factor for each column of a multiplication table is one more than the column left of it.

![The same table as above, with the columns labeled 6, 7, and 8 respectively.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e8747e2e5030bf6ab2dbbad87e45f9f8ad692c1)

Now we know the factors, so we can multiply to find the missing numbers.

$4 \times 7 = 28$

![The same table as above, with intersection of row 4 and column 7 labeled 28.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6a6985f58b2d4d521b1f093f4445de82e066e3b3)

$5 \times 7 = 35$

![The same table as above, with intersection of row 5 and column 7 labeled 35.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ea38146ded6cbf6cf1bf90fcc0539f0319adcbb3)

Here is the completed pattern.

items.question_content:
**Which statement describes a pattern in the shaded parts of the multiplication table?**

![Multiplication table with the column and row labeled 6 shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/896c72986888130b0f6f6a5a3ff2faf72190e827)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's think about each statement to see whether it is true.

We read the multiplication table from left to right and from top to bottom.

###We double each number to get the next number.

$\begin{align}
6\blueE{\times 2}&\stackrel{\checkmark}{=}12\\\\
12\blueE{\times 2}&=24 \neq18\
\end{align}$

This statement is not true.

###If a number is even, then the next number is odd.

$6$ is even. The next number, $12$, is also even.

This statement is not true.

###If we change the order of the factors, we get the same product.

$\begin{align}
6 \times 1 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 1 \times 6 = 6\\\\
6 \times 2 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 2 \times 6 = 12\\\\
6 \times 3 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 3 \times 6 = 18\\\\
6 \times 4 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 4 \times 6 = 24\\\\
6 \times 5 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 5 \times 6 = 30\\\\
6 \times 6 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 6 \times 6 = 36\\\\
6 \times 7 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 7 \times 6 = 42\\\\
6 \times 8 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 8 \times 6 = 48\\\\
6 \times 9 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 9 \times 6 = 54\\\\
6 \times 10 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} 10 \times 6 = 60
\end{align}$

This statement is true.

This statement is true:

- If we change the order of the factors, we get the same product.

choices.content: We double each shaded number to get the next shaded number.
If a shaded number is even, then the next shaded number is odd.
If we change the order of the factors of each shaded number, we get the same product.

items.question_content:
**Which statement describes a pattern in the shaded parts of the multiplication table?**

![Multiplication table with the column and row labeled 5 shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/231e913873fdeaee9cef316d0870843428244d68)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's think about each statement to see whether it is true.

We read the multiplication table from left to right and from top to bottom.

###Each number has a $0$ or $5$ in the ones place.

$\begin{align}
5&=0\text{ tens}+5\text{ ones}\\\\
10&=1\text{ tens}+0\text{ ones}\\\\
15&=1\text{ tens}+5\text{ ones}\\\\
20&=2\text{ tens}+0\text{ ones}\\\\
25&=2\text{ tens}+5\text{ ones}\\\\
30&=3\text{ tens}+0\text{ ones}\\\\
35&=3\text{ tens}+5\text{ ones}\\\\
40&=4\text{ tens}+0\text{ ones}\\\\
45&=4\text{ tens}+5\text{ ones}\\\\
50&=5\text{ tens}+0\text{ ones}
\end{align}$

This statement is true.

###Each number has a $0$ or $5$ in the tens place.

The number $5$ has a $0$ in the tens place. The next number, $10$, has a $1$ in the tens place.

This statement is not true.

###The ones digit and the tens digit of each number add up to a multiple of $5$.

The number $5$ has $0$ tens and $5$ ones. Its digits add up to $5$.

The number $10$ has $1$ ten and $0$ ones. Its digits add up to $1$. The number $1$ is not a multiple of $5$.

This statement is not true.

This statement is true:

- Each number has a $0$ or $5$ in the ones place.

choices.content: Each shaded number has a $0$ or $5$ in the ones place.
Each shaded number has a $0$ or $5$ in the tens place.
The ones digit and the tens digit of each shaded number add up to a multiple of $5$.

items.question_content:
**Which statement describes a pattern in the shaded parts of the multiplication table?**

![Multiplication table with the diagonal from 1 times 1 to 10 times 10 shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf6657bf94959ff493d5900fc23fab423f0a2b90)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's think about each statement to see whether it is true.

We read the multiplication table from left to right and from top to bottom.

###Each shaded number is larger than the two numbers we multiply to make it.

$1=1 \times 1$

The first shaded number, $1$, is equal to the numbers we multiply to make it, not greater.

This statement is not true.

###We add $3$ to each number to get the next number.

$\begin{align}
1\purpleE{+3}&\stackrel{\checkmark}{=}4\\\\
4\purpleE{+3}&\neq 9\\\\
9\purpleE{+3}&\neq 16
\end{align}$

This statement is not true.

###We multiply a number times itself to get each shaded number.

$\begin{align}
1 \times 1 &= 1\\\\
2 \times 2 &= 4\\\\
3 \times 3 &= 9\\\\
4 \times 4 &= 16\\\\
5 \times 5 &= 25\\\\
6 \times 6 &= 36\\\\
7 \times 7 &= 49\\\\
8 \times 8 &= 64\\\\
9 \times 9 &= 81\\\\
10 \times 10 &= 100
\end{align}$

This statement is true.

This statement is true:

- We multiply a number times itself to get each shaded number.

choices.content: We multiply a number times itself to get each shaded number.
Each shaded number is larger than the two numbers we multiply to make it.
We add $3$ to each shaded number to get the next shaded number.

items.question_content:
**Which statement describes a pattern in the shaded parts of the multiplication table?**

![Multiplication table from 1 times 1 through 10 times 10, with the row labeled 9 shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4f3e3e9c1a3c3b5da523540f09120ab5e4891dec)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's think about each statement to see whether it is true.

We read the multiplication table from left to right and from top to bottom.

###Each number is odd.

The number $9$ is odd, but the number $18$ is even.

This statement is not true.

###The ones digit and the tens digit of each number add up to a multiple of $9$.

Some multiples of $9$ are $9$, $18$, and $27$.

||
-:|:-:
$9=0\text{ tens}+9\text{ ones}$ | $0+9=9$
$18=1\text{ tens}+8\text{ ones}$ | $1+8=9$
$27=2\text{ tens}+7\text{ ones}$ | $2+7=9$
$36=3\text{ tens}+6\text{ ones}$ | $3+6=9$
$45=4\text{ tens}+5\text{ ones}$ | $4+5=9$
$54=5\text{ tens}+4\text{ ones}$ | $5+4=9$
$63=6\text{ tens}+3\text{ ones}$ | $6+3=9$
$72=7\text{ tens}+2\text{ ones}$ | $7+2=9$
$81=8\text{ tens}+1\text{ ones}$ | $8+1=9$
$90=9\text{ tens}+0\text{ ones}$ | $9+0=9$
$99=9\text{ tens}+9\text{ ones}$ | $9+9=18$

This statement is true.

###The ones digit is $1$ less than the tens digit.

In the number $18$, the ones digit, $8$, is $7$ more than the tens digit, $1$.

This statement is not true.

This statement is true:

- The ones digit and the tens digit of each number add up to a multiple of $9$.

choices.content: The ones digit and the tens digit of each shaded number add up to a multiple of $9$.
The ones digit is $1$ less than the tens digit in each shaded number.

items.question_content:
Look at the pattern below:

$2, 6, 10, 14, ...$

**If the pattern continues, what will be the $6^{\text{th}}$ number in the pattern?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the pattern that connects each number.

$2\underset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}6\underset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 10 \underset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 14$

The pattern starts at $2$ and adds $4$ each time.

$2\underset{\blueD{+~4}}{{\rightarrow}}6\underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} 10 \underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} 14$

So, if we want to know what the $6$th number will be, we need to extend the pattern:

$\overset{\greenD1}2\underset{\blueD{+~4}}{{\rightarrow}}\overset{\greenD2}6\underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} \overset{\greenD3}{10} \underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} \overset{\greenD4} {14} \underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} \overset{\greenD5}{18} \underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} \overset{\greenD6}{22}$
The $6^{\text{th}}$ number in the pattern will be $22$.

items.question_content:
**Which expression can we use to find the missing number in the pattern?**

$36, 45, \_\_\_, 63, 72, 81$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the pattern that connects each number.

$36\overset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}45\overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} ~? \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 63\overset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}72 \overset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}81$

The pattern is to start at $36$ and add $9$ each time.

$36\overset{\blueD{+~9}}{{\rightarrow}}45\overset{\blueD{+~9}}{\rightarrow} ~\goldD? \overset{\blueD{+~9}}{\rightarrow} 63\overset{\blueD{+~9}}{{\rightarrow}}72 \overset{\blueD{+~9}}{{\rightarrow}}81$

To find the missing number, we need to $\blueD{\text{add 9}}$ to $45$. 

$36{{\rightarrow}}45\overset{\blueD{+~9}}{\rightarrow} ~\goldD{54} {\rightarrow} 63{{\rightarrow}}72 {{\rightarrow}}81$
The expression that helps us find the missing number is $45 + 9$.

The pattern is to start at $30$ and add $15$ each time.

$30\overset{\blueD{+~15}}{{\longrightarrow}}45\overset{\blueD{+~15}}{{\longrightarrow}} 60 \overset{\blueD{+~15}}{\longrightarrow} \goldD?\overset{\blueD{+~15}}{{\longrightarrow}}90 \overset{\blueD{+~15}}{{\longrightarrow}}105$

To find the missing number, we need to $\blueD{\text{add 15}}$ to $60$. 

$30{{\longrightarrow}}45{\longrightarrow} ~{60}\overset{\blueD{+~15}}{\longrightarrow} \goldD{75}{{\longrightarrow}}90 {{\longrightarrow}}105$
The expression that helps us find the missing number is $60 + 15$.

The pattern is to start at $3$ and multiply by $2$ each time.

$3\overset{\blueD{\times2}}{{\rightarrow}}6\overset{\blueD{\times2}}{\rightarrow} ~12 \overset{\blueD{\times2}}{\rightarrow} \goldD?\overset{\blueD{\times2}}{{\rightarrow}}48 \overset{\blueD{\times2}}{{\rightarrow}}96$

To find the missing number, we need to $\blueD{\text{multiply 2}}$ and $12$. 

$3{{\rightarrow}}6{\rightarrow} ~{12}\overset{\blueD{\times2}}{\rightarrow} \goldD{24}{{\rightarrow}}48 {{\rightarrow}}96$
The expression that helps us find the missing number is $12\times2$.

Look at the pattern below:

$2, 6, 10, 14, ...$

**If the pattern continues, what will be the $6^{\text{th}}$ number in the pattern?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the pattern that connects each number.

$2\underset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}6\underset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 10 \underset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 14$

The pattern starts at $2$ and adds $4$ each time.

$2\underset{\blueD{+~4}}{{\rightarrow}}6\underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} 10 \underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} 14$

So, if we want to know what the $6$th number will be, we need to extend the pattern:

$\overset{\greenD1}2\underset{\blueD{+~4}}{{\rightarrow}}\overset{\greenD2}6\underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} \overset{\greenD3}{10} \underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} \overset{\greenD4} {14} \underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} \overset{\greenD5}{18} \underset{\blueD{+~4}}{\rightarrow} \overset{\greenD6}{22}$
The $6^{\text{th}}$ number in the pattern will be $22$.

$36\overset{\blueD{+~9}}{{\rightarrow}}45\overset{\blueD{+~9}}{\rightarrow} ~\goldD? \overset{\blueD{+~9}}{\rightarrow} 63\overset{\blueD{+~9}}{{\rightarrow}}72 \overset{\blueD{+~9}}{{\rightarrow}}81$

To find the missing number, we need to $\blueD{\text{add 9}}$ to $45$. 

$36{{\rightarrow}}45\overset{\blueD{+~9}}{\rightarrow} ~\goldD{54} {\rightarrow} 63{{\rightarrow}}72 {{\rightarrow}}81$
The expression that helps us find the missing number is $45 + 9$.

items.question_content:
**Which of the following have an even sum?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An $\blueD{\text{even}}$ number is a multiple of $2$. It can be split into $2$ equal groups.
[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD2$, $\blueD4$, $\blueD6$, $\blueD8$, $\blueD{10}$,  $. .  .$

An $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number is **not** a multiple of $2$. It **cannot** be split into $2$ equal groups. 

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD1$, $\maroonD3$, $\maroonD5$, $\maroonD7$, $\maroonD9$, $. . . $

$\blueD{\text{even}}$ number $+$ $\blueD{\text{even}}$ number  $=$ $\blueD{\text{even}}$ number 


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number $+$  $\blueD{\text{even}}$ number $=$ $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number 

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number $+$ $\maroonD{\text{odd}}$ number  $=$ $\blueD{\text{even}}$ number 

[[☃ image 1]]

The following have even sums:

 - $\blueD2 + \blueD{12}$  
 -  $\maroonD{5\text{ cows}} + \maroonD{3\text{ cows}}$

khan_exercises.title: 2-step word problems
khan_exercises.description: Solve two-step word problems with addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.  Some questions include estimation.

items.question_content:
I put $75$ jelly beans in a bowl. Each day I eat $6$ jelly beans.
 
**How many jelly beans  are in the bowl after $9$ days?** 
 
[[☃ input-number 1]] jelly beans
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]






I eat $6$ jelly beans each day for $9$ days.

We can multiply to find the number of jelly beans eaten. 

$9\times6=\blueD{54}$

$\greenD{\text{Total}}$ jelly beans $-$ jelly beans $\blueD{\text{eaten}}$ $=$ number  left in the $\maroonD{\text{bowl}}$ 



$\greenD{75}-\blueD{54}=\maroonD{21}$
$\maroonD{21}$ jelly beans are in the bowl after $9$ days.

items.question_content:
I used $9$ sheets of toilet paper each day for $7$ days. The roll had $96$ sheets when it was new. 
 
**How many sheets of toilet paper  have not been used?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] sheets
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]



I used $9$ sheets of toilet paper each day for $7$ days.

We can multiply to find the number of sheets used.
 
I have used $7\times9=\blueD{63}$ sheets.

$\greenD{\text{Total}}$ sheets of paper $-$ sheets of paper $\blueD{\text{used}}$ $=$ number of sheets left on the $\maroonD{\text{roll}}$ 



$\greenD{96}-\blueD{63}=\maroonD{33}$
$\maroonD{33}$ sheets have not been used.

items.question_content:
It costs $\$120$ to have a party for $9$ people at the skating rink. The cost includes a charge of $\$10$ for each person attending the party plus the price of a cake.

**How much does the cake cost?**    

$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]

There are $9$ people at the party and each paid $\$10$.

We can multiply to find the cost for the people at the party. 

The cost for the party guests is $9\times\$10=\$\blueD{90}$.

$\greenD{\text{Total}}$ cost of the party $-$ costs of the $\blueD{\text{guests}}$ $=$ cost of the  $\maroonD{\text{cake}}$. 



$\greenD{120}-\blueD{90}=\maroonD{30}$
The cake is $\$\maroonD{30}$.

items.question_content:
My book is $131$ pages. I have been reading $7$ pages each day for $8$ days.

**How many pages are left to read?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] pages
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]


I have read $7$ pages each day for $8$ days.

We can multiply to find the number of pages read so far.

$8\times7=\blueD{56}$

$\greenD{\text{Total}}$ pages $-$ pages $\blueD{\text{read}}$ $=$ pages $\maroonD{\text{left}}$ to read

$\greenD{131}-\blueD{56}=\maroonD{75}$
$\maroonD{75}$ pages are left to read.

items.question_content:
Our skating club is going on a field trip. To cover the cost of the trip we are collecting $\$8$ from each of the $9$ members of the club. We also raised $\$178$ at a car wash. 

**How much money have we raised for our trip all together?**
  
$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]



$9$ members each paid $\$8$.

We can multiply to find the amount collected from members.   

$9\times8=\$\blueD{72}$

Amount from $\blueD{\text{members}}$ $+$ amount from $\maroonD{\text{car wash}}$ $=$ $\greenD{\text{total money raised}}$

$\blueD{72} + \maroonD{178}= \greenD{250}$
We have raised $\$250$ for our trip.

items.question_content:
I have $47$ marbles. Then, $6$ of my friends each give me $7$ marbles.
 
**How many marbles  do I have?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] marbles
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]





$6$ of my friends each give me $7$ marbles.

We can multiply to find the total number of marbles from friends. 

$6\times7=\blueD{42}$

Marbles $\blueD{\text{from friends}}$ $+$  $\maroonD{\text{starting}}$ marbles $=$ $\greenD{\text{total}}$ marbles
 
$\blueD{42}+\maroonD{47}=\greenD{89}$
I have $89$ marbles.

items.question_content:
Mabel had a skating rink party for her birthday. The cost to rent the party room was $\$75$.  The cost of the ticket for each of the $6$ people who came to the party was $\$8$.

**What was the total cost of the party?**  

$\$$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]


$6$ people each paid $8$ for a ticket.

We can multiply to find the total cost of the tickets. 

$6\times8=\blueD{48}$

Cost of  $\blueD{\text{tickets}}$ $+$ cost of  $\maroonD{\text{party room}}$ $=$ $\greenD{\text{total}} $ cost

$\blueD{48} +\maroonD{75} = \greenD{123}$
The total cost of the party was $\$123$.

items.question_content:
I had a jar of jelly beans that weighed $56$ ounces.  I added $16$ more ounces of jelly beans to the jar. Then I divided the jelly beans evenly into bags that each weighed $8$ ounces.
 
**How many bags of jelly beans did I make?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] bags
hints:
We can add to find the total amount of jelly beans.  

$56+16=72$ ounces

Now we can divide the total ounces into groups with $8$ ounces  in each group.

$72 \div 8 = 9$
I made $9$ bags of jelly beans.

items.question_content:
I had $42$ ounces of rice. On Monday I bought $58$ more ounces of rice. Then I divided all the rice into $10$ equal-sized portions for dinner with friends.
 
**How many ounces  of rice were in each portion?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] ounces
hints:
We can add to find the total weight of the rice.  

$42+58=100$ ounces

Now we can divide the total amount of rice into $10$ equal-sized groups.

$100 \div 10 = 10$
Each portion had $10$ ounces of rice.

$57+15=$$\$72$

$72\div9=8$
Each movie ticket cost $\$8$.
